  • Women express greater concerns about retirement finances than men
  • Downsizing remains more popular than equity release for addressing shortfalls

These findings underscore the complex relationship between property ownership and retirement planning in the UK. With many homeowners potentially overlooking housing wealth as part of their retirement strategy, financial advisers might highlight the importance of considering all assets when planning for later life.

item.title: Chestertons boosts Caribbean presence
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

International estate agency Chestertons has extended its global reach with two new strategic partnerships in the Caribbean property market. The firm has established operations in St Vincent and the Grenadines alongside the Cayman Islands, marking a significant expansion of its overseas network.

Strategic Caribbean expansion

The move forms part of Chestertons' ongoing strategy to strengthen its presence in key international property markets. By partnering with established local agencies in these Caribbean locations, the company gains immediate access to prime real estate markets favoured by British investors and second-home buyers.

St Vincent and the Grenadines offers a growing luxury property sector, whilst the Cayman Islands remains one of the most stable Caribbean markets with strong demand from overseas buyers. This dual expansion allows Chestertons to cater to different segments of the international property market.

Opportunities for UK investors

For UK-based property investors considering overseas opportunities, Chestertons' expansion could provide:

  • Improved access to Caribbean property markets through a familiar UK brand
  • Local market expertise combined with international brokerage experience
  • Potential for portfolio diversification in stable, dollar-pegged economies

Those exploring international property investment might wish to consider that Caribbean markets often offer different risk profiles and tax implications compared to UK buy-to-let investments. Professional financial advice is typically recommended when purchasing property abroad.

As global property markets continue to evolve, established UK agencies expanding their international networks may provide British investors with more options for overseas real estate opportunities. The Caribbean remains a popular choice for those seeking holiday homes or investment properties in favourable climates.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

International estate agency Chestertons has extended its global reach with two new strategic partnerships in the Caribbean property market. The firm has established operations in St Vincent and the Grenadines alongside the Cayman Islands, marking a significant expansion of its overseas network.

Strategic Caribbean expansion

The move forms part of Chestertons' ongoing strategy to strengthen its presence in key international property markets. By partnering with established local agencies in these Caribbean locations, the company gains immediate access to prime real estate markets favoured by British investors and second-home buyers.

St Vincent and the Grenadines offers a growing luxury property sector, whilst the Cayman Islands remains one of the most stable Caribbean markets with strong demand from overseas buyers. This dual expansion allows Chestertons to cater to different segments of the international property market.

Opportunities for UK investors

For UK-based property investors considering overseas opportunities, Chestertons' expansion could provide:

  • Improved access to Caribbean property markets through a familiar UK brand
  • Local market expertise combined with international brokerage experience
  • Potential for portfolio diversification in stable, dollar-pegged economies

Those exploring international property investment might wish to consider that Caribbean markets often offer different risk profiles and tax implications compared to UK buy-to-let investments. Professional financial advice is typically recommended when purchasing property abroad.

As global property markets continue to evolve, established UK agencies expanding their international networks may provide British investors with more options for overseas real estate opportunities. The Caribbean remains a popular choice for those seeking holiday homes or investment properties in favourable climates.

item.title: Egypt overlooked by property investors
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Egypt's property market may be flying under the radar for many UK investors, despite showing promising signs of growth potential. With comparatively lower entry prices and a rapidly expanding population, the North African country could present an alternative opportunity for those looking to diversify their property portfolios internationally.

Why Egypt's property market merits attention

Several factors make Egypt's real estate sector worthy of consideration:

  • Property prices remain relatively affordable compared to many established markets
  • The country's young and growing population continues to drive housing demand
  • Major infrastructure projects are transforming urban areas
  • The government has introduced reforms to encourage foreign investment

Important considerations for potential investors

Whilst the Egyptian market shows potential, UK investors might want to carefully weigh several aspects before committing:

  • Currency fluctuations between the Egyptian pound and sterling could affect returns
  • Local property laws and taxation systems differ significantly from the UK
  • Political and economic stability should be thoroughly researched
  • Practicalities of managing overseas properties may require local partnerships

As with any international property investment, thorough due diligence and professional advice remain essential. The Egyptian market's current affordability and demographic trends could make it worth monitoring for investors seeking emerging market opportunities beyond traditional European destinations.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Egypt's property market may be flying under the radar for many UK investors, despite showing promising signs of growth potential. With comparatively lower entry prices and a rapidly expanding population, the North African country could present an alternative opportunity for those looking to diversify their property portfolios internationally.

Why Egypt's property market merits attention

Several factors make Egypt's real estate sector worthy of consideration:

  • Property prices remain relatively affordable compared to many established markets
  • The country's young and growing population continues to drive housing demand
  • Major infrastructure projects are transforming urban areas
  • The government has introduced reforms to encourage foreign investment

Important considerations for potential investors

Whilst the Egyptian market shows potential, UK investors might want to carefully weigh several aspects before committing:

  • Currency fluctuations between the Egyptian pound and sterling could affect returns
  • Local property laws and taxation systems differ significantly from the UK
  • Political and economic stability should be thoroughly researched
  • Practicalities of managing overseas properties may require local partnerships

As with any international property investment, thorough due diligence and professional advice remain essential. The Egyptian market's current affordability and demographic trends could make it worth monitoring for investors seeking emerging market opportunities beyond traditional European destinations.

item.title: Rent-to-Rent: What Every Landlord Needs to Know
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Rent-to-rent arrangements have grown in popularity amongst UK landlords seeking steady rental income without the responsibilities of direct tenant management. Under these agreements, a third party – often a letting agent or property management firm – leases the property from the landlord and assumes control over finding tenants and handling day-to-day operations. Whilst this hands-off approach may appeal to some property owners, there are several important considerations to weigh before entering such an arrangement.

How Rent-to-Rent Works

In a typical rent-to-rent scenario, the intermediary becomes the landlord's tenant, paying a fixed monthly rent regardless of whether they've found occupants for the property. The key features include:

  • The intermediary takes responsibility for finding and managing sub-tenants
  • Landlords receive guaranteed rental payments, often slightly below market rate
  • Property owners avoid direct involvement with tenant issues or void periods
  • Contracts typically run for 3-5 years with break clauses

This model is particularly common with Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMOs) and serviced accommodation, where the intermediary may refurbish the property to increase its rental potential.

Key Considerations for Landlords

Whilst rent-to-rent can offer convenience, landlords might wish to carefully evaluate several factors:

  • Legal compliance: Some arrangements may inadvertently create a licence rather than a tenancy, affecting eviction rights
  • Mortgage terms: Many buy-to-let mortgages prohibit subletting without lender consent
  • Insurance implications: Standard landlord policies may not cover rent-to-rent scenarios
  • Operator reliability: Thorough due diligence on the intermediary's track record is essential

Property owners may also want to consider how the arrangement affects their tax position, particularly regarding business rates and VAT obligations if the property is converted to serviced accommodation.

As with any property arrangement, professional legal advice could prove invaluable before committing to a rent-to-rent agreement. Landlords might find it beneficial to weigh the convenience of guaranteed income against the potential loss of control and flexibility over their asset.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Rent-to-rent arrangements have grown in popularity amongst UK landlords seeking steady rental income without the responsibilities of direct tenant management. Under these agreements, a third party – often a letting agent or property management firm – leases the property from the landlord and assumes control over finding tenants and handling day-to-day operations. Whilst this hands-off approach may appeal to some property owners, there are several important considerations to weigh before entering such an arrangement.

How Rent-to-Rent Works

In a typical rent-to-rent scenario, the intermediary becomes the landlord's tenant, paying a fixed monthly rent regardless of whether they've found occupants for the property. The key features include:

  • The intermediary takes responsibility for finding and managing sub-tenants
  • Landlords receive guaranteed rental payments, often slightly below market rate
  • Property owners avoid direct involvement with tenant issues or void periods
  • Contracts typically run for 3-5 years with break clauses

This model is particularly common with Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMOs) and serviced accommodation, where the intermediary may refurbish the property to increase its rental potential.

Key Considerations for Landlords

Whilst rent-to-rent can offer convenience, landlords might wish to carefully evaluate several factors:

  • Legal compliance: Some arrangements may inadvertently create a licence rather than a tenancy, affecting eviction rights
  • Mortgage terms: Many buy-to-let mortgages prohibit subletting without lender consent
  • Insurance implications: Standard landlord policies may not cover rent-to-rent scenarios
  • Operator reliability: Thorough due diligence on the intermediary's track record is essential

Property owners may also want to consider how the arrangement affects their tax position, particularly regarding business rates and VAT obligations if the property is converted to serviced accommodation.

As with any property arrangement, professional legal advice could prove invaluable before committing to a rent-to-rent agreement. Landlords might find it beneficial to weigh the convenience of guaranteed income against the potential loss of control and flexibility over their asset.

item.title: Farage proposal could drive thousands into private rental market
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Reform UK leader Nigel Farage has recently unveiled a series of controversial housing and immigration proposals, which could have significant implications for the UK’s social and private rental markets. Among the key suggestions is a plan to ban foreign nationals from living in social housing, with existing tenants required to transition to private rented accommodation within three months. The policy would apply retrospectively, affecting individuals regardless of how long they have resided in the UK. This proposal forms part of a broader pledge by Reform UK to abolish Indefinite Leave to Remain.

The Impact on Social Housing

Social housing providers are already grappling with high demand, with approximately 1.34 million households currently on waiting lists for a home. Farage’s proposal, if implemented, could place additional strain on these already stretched resources. He argues that social housing should be reserved exclusively for British citizens, a stance that has sparked debate across the housing sector. Critics have raised concerns about the practical implications for social landlords, local authorities, and tenants currently relying on social housing.

A government spokesperson highlighted that existing rules already prioritise UK nationals for social housing, with nearly 90% of social homes allocated to British citizens. Local connection requirements also ensure that housing goes to those with strong ties to the area, such as social care workers or nurses at local hospitals. However, Reform UK’s proposals suggest a more stringent approach, which could lead to significant upheaval for many households.

Challenges for the Private Rental Market

The potential large-scale movement of tenants from social housing to the private rental sector raises concerns about its capacity to absorb additional demand. The private rental market is already facing challenges, including limited supply and rising rents in many parts of the country. Should the policy be enacted, it could exacerbate these issues, making it even harder for tenants to secure affordable accommodation.

Sarah Elliott, chief executive of housing charity Shelter, warned that such measures could lead to increased homelessness and housing instability. The proposal has also drawn criticism for its retrospective application, which could displace individuals who have lived in the UK for years, regardless of their circumstances.

Broader Immigration and Equality Concerns

Farage’s essay, published on Substack, goes beyond housing policy, touching on wider immigration and equality issues. He criticised existing equality legislation, describing it as a form of “social cleansing” and claiming that “anti-white racism” is embedded within public institutions. These comments have sparked further debate, with many questioning the implications of such rhetoric for social cohesion.

Reform UK has also pledged to renegotiate aspects of post-Brexit agreements covering citizens’ rights, particularly where it believes current arrangements lack reciprocity between the UK and European Union member states. This stance underscores the party’s broader focus on immigration reform, which remains a contentious topic in British politics.

As the debate around these proposals continues, stakeholders across the housing sector will be closely monitoring their potential impact. With social housing demand already high and the private rental market under pressure, any significant policy changes could have far-reaching consequences for tenants and landlords alike.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Reform UK leader Nigel Farage has recently unveiled a series of controversial housing and immigration proposals, which could have significant implications for the UK’s social and private rental markets. Among the key suggestions is a plan to ban foreign nationals from living in social housing, with existing tenants required to transition to private rented accommodation within three months. The policy would apply retrospectively, affecting individuals regardless of how long they have resided in the UK. This proposal forms part of a broader pledge by Reform UK to abolish Indefinite Leave to Remain.

The Impact on Social Housing

Social housing providers are already grappling with high demand, with approximately 1.34 million households currently on waiting lists for a home. Farage’s proposal, if implemented, could place additional strain on these already stretched resources. He argues that social housing should be reserved exclusively for British citizens, a stance that has sparked debate across the housing sector. Critics have raised concerns about the practical implications for social landlords, local authorities, and tenants currently relying on social housing.

A government spokesperson highlighted that existing rules already prioritise UK nationals for social housing, with nearly 90% of social homes allocated to British citizens. Local connection requirements also ensure that housing goes to those with strong ties to the area, such as social care workers or nurses at local hospitals. However, Reform UK’s proposals suggest a more stringent approach, which could lead to significant upheaval for many households.

Challenges for the Private Rental Market

The potential large-scale movement of tenants from social housing to the private rental sector raises concerns about its capacity to absorb additional demand. The private rental market is already facing challenges, including limited supply and rising rents in many parts of the country. Should the policy be enacted, it could exacerbate these issues, making it even harder for tenants to secure affordable accommodation.

Sarah Elliott, chief executive of housing charity Shelter, warned that such measures could lead to increased homelessness and housing instability. The proposal has also drawn criticism for its retrospective application, which could displace individuals who have lived in the UK for years, regardless of their circumstances.

Broader Immigration and Equality Concerns

Farage’s essay, published on Substack, goes beyond housing policy, touching on wider immigration and equality issues. He criticised existing equality legislation, describing it as a form of “social cleansing” and claiming that “anti-white racism” is embedded within public institutions. These comments have sparked further debate, with many questioning the implications of such rhetoric for social cohesion.

Reform UK has also pledged to renegotiate aspects of post-Brexit agreements covering citizens’ rights, particularly where it believes current arrangements lack reciprocity between the UK and European Union member states. This stance underscores the party’s broader focus on immigration reform, which remains a contentious topic in British politics.

As the debate around these proposals continues, stakeholders across the housing sector will be closely monitoring their potential impact. With social housing demand already high and the private rental market under pressure, any significant policy changes could have far-reaching consequences for tenants and landlords alike.

item.title: Eviction notices surged ahead of Section 21 abolition, new data confirms
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Landlords issued more eviction notices ahead of Section 21 abolition

New data reveals a significant rise in eviction notices served to tenants in the months leading up to the abolition of Section 21 no-fault evictions. Analysis of 150,000 tenancies shows nearly one in four renters received notices in the final month before the Renters’ Rights Act came into force on 1 May 2026.

Property management firm COHO estimates the reforms contributed to around 73,900 additional tenancy notices since plans were first announced in 2023. The proportion of affected tenancies peaked at 27.1% immediately before implementation – a sharp increase from the historic average of 5.7%.

Why landlords acted before the reforms

The data suggests landlords adjusted their approach as the regulatory landscape changed. Notice levels initially rose to 8% after the Renters’ Rights Act was proposed, climbing further to 11.4% following the general election when the legislation’s passage became more certain.

COHO’s CEO Vann Vogstad explained: “Section 21 gave landlords a safety net. Without it, dealing with serious rent arrears or anti-social behaviour could take months through the courts. Some landlords have acted pre-emptively to avoid being left without recourse.”

However, experts caution against interpreting this solely as a wave of evictions. Many landlords continue working with tenants facing difficulties, as empty properties generate no income. The figures may instead reflect a rebalancing of risk assessments in the private rental sector.

What the changes mean for the rental market

The reforms mark a fundamental shift in landlord-tenant relationships. Whilst Section 21 allowed relatively quick property repossessions, its removal means landlords must now rely on slower court processes for most evictions.

Industry observers suggest the spike in notices represents landlords adapting to the new environment rather than widespread tenant displacement. As the market adjusts, both landlords and tenants may need to reconsider their approaches to tenancy agreements and property maintenance.

The full impact of these reforms will become clearer in coming months as the rental sector adapts to the post-Section 21 landscape. Both landlords and tenants may wish to review their rights and responsibilities under the new legislation.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Landlords issued more eviction notices ahead of Section 21 abolition

New data reveals a significant rise in eviction notices served to tenants in the months leading up to the abolition of Section 21 no-fault evictions. Analysis of 150,000 tenancies shows nearly one in four renters received notices in the final month before the Renters’ Rights Act came into force on 1 May 2026.

Property management firm COHO estimates the reforms contributed to around 73,900 additional tenancy notices since plans were first announced in 2023. The proportion of affected tenancies peaked at 27.1% immediately before implementation – a sharp increase from the historic average of 5.7%.

Why landlords acted before the reforms

The data suggests landlords adjusted their approach as the regulatory landscape changed. Notice levels initially rose to 8% after the Renters’ Rights Act was proposed, climbing further to 11.4% following the general election when the legislation’s passage became more certain.

COHO’s CEO Vann Vogstad explained: “Section 21 gave landlords a safety net. Without it, dealing with serious rent arrears or anti-social behaviour could take months through the courts. Some landlords have acted pre-emptively to avoid being left without recourse.”

However, experts caution against interpreting this solely as a wave of evictions. Many landlords continue working with tenants facing difficulties, as empty properties generate no income. The figures may instead reflect a rebalancing of risk assessments in the private rental sector.

What the changes mean for the rental market

The reforms mark a fundamental shift in landlord-tenant relationships. Whilst Section 21 allowed relatively quick property repossessions, its removal means landlords must now rely on slower court processes for most evictions.

Industry observers suggest the spike in notices represents landlords adapting to the new environment rather than widespread tenant displacement. As the market adjusts, both landlords and tenants may need to reconsider their approaches to tenancy agreements and property maintenance.

The full impact of these reforms will become clearer in coming months as the rental sector adapts to the post-Section 21 landscape. Both landlords and tenants may wish to review their rights and responsibilities under the new legislation.

item.title: Estate agents hit the ground running
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Over 50 property professionals laced up their trainers for the inaugural Propertymark One Fun Run, raising more than £2,000 for The Propertymark Trust. The 5km charity event, held ahead of the organisation's annual conference, brought together participants of all fitness levels – from competitive runners to enthusiastic walkers – united in supporting the industry's charitable foundation.

A roaring success

The early morning run saw delegates swap business suits for running gear, with one participant completing the course in a full dinosaur costume. The light-hearted approach helped attract attention and additional donations throughout the day. Funds were boosted by £1,000 generated directly from the run, supplemented by sales of mental health workbooks by David Mintz and signed copies of Hacking Humanity donated by keynote speaker Lara Lewington.

Participants received commemorative medals upon finishing, which became a visible symbol of achievement during subsequent conference sessions. The event created lasting camaraderie among attendees, with many spotted proudly wearing their medals throughout the day's proceedings.

Industry spirit on display

Angharad Trueman, trustee and group lettings director at Andrews Property Group, commented: "The response exceeded all expectations. Seeing colleagues encouraging one another around the course – from serious athletes to our dinosaur-clad participant – perfectly captured the inclusive nature of our sector."

Megan Eighteen, another Propertymark Trust trustee, added: "The event fostered genuine connections before the conference began. The sight of professionals embracing the challenge, coupled with the ongoing support for our mental health initiatives, demonstrates the compassionate side of our industry."

Organisers hope the fun run will become an annual tradition, building on this year's success in combining fitness, fundraising and professional networking. The funds raised will support The Propertymark Trust's work in addressing homelessness and improving living standards across the UK property sector.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Over 50 property professionals laced up their trainers for the inaugural Propertymark One Fun Run, raising more than £2,000 for The Propertymark Trust. The 5km charity event, held ahead of the organisation's annual conference, brought together participants of all fitness levels – from competitive runners to enthusiastic walkers – united in supporting the industry's charitable foundation.

A roaring success

The early morning run saw delegates swap business suits for running gear, with one participant completing the course in a full dinosaur costume. The light-hearted approach helped attract attention and additional donations throughout the day. Funds were boosted by £1,000 generated directly from the run, supplemented by sales of mental health workbooks by David Mintz and signed copies of Hacking Humanity donated by keynote speaker Lara Lewington.

Participants received commemorative medals upon finishing, which became a visible symbol of achievement during subsequent conference sessions. The event created lasting camaraderie among attendees, with many spotted proudly wearing their medals throughout the day's proceedings.

Industry spirit on display

Angharad Trueman, trustee and group lettings director at Andrews Property Group, commented: "The response exceeded all expectations. Seeing colleagues encouraging one another around the course – from serious athletes to our dinosaur-clad participant – perfectly captured the inclusive nature of our sector."

Megan Eighteen, another Propertymark Trust trustee, added: "The event fostered genuine connections before the conference began. The sight of professionals embracing the challenge, coupled with the ongoing support for our mental health initiatives, demonstrates the compassionate side of our industry."

Organisers hope the fun run will become an annual tradition, building on this year's success in combining fitness, fundraising and professional networking. The funds raised will support The Propertymark Trust's work in addressing homelessness and improving living standards across the UK property sector.

item.title: House price growth shifts north as London falls behind
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Northern Cities Lead UK House Price Growth as London Lags Behind

New analysis reveals a striking regional divide in UK property price growth over the past decade, with Manchester emerging as the standout performer while London trails behind. According to Rightmove data, average asking prices in Manchester have surged by 63% since 2014, compared to just 7% growth in the capital. This widening gap highlights how affordability and shifting work patterns are reshaping Britain's housing market.

The North-South Price Growth Divide

The research shows none of the top 10 cities for house price growth are located in southern England, with Greater Manchester communities dominating the list. Four areas - Levenshulme, Atherton, Droylsden and Failsworth - have each seen prices climb around 80% since 2016. Other strong performers include Wolverhampton, Newport and Nottingham, all benefiting from their relative affordability compared to southern counterparts.

Several factors appear to be driving this trend:

  • More buyers seeking value beyond traditional expensive urban centres
  • A "spillover" effect as purchasers look to neighbouring towns and cities
  • Changing work patterns reducing the need to live near major employment hubs

London's Slower Growth Trajectory

While London retains the UK's highest average asking prices at £687,080, its modest 7% growth over the decade suggests the capital's market may be approaching affordability limits. Rightmove's analysis indicates that with prices already at elevated levels, London has less capacity for further significant increases compared to more affordable regions.

The data also reveals:

  • Five of the 10 slowest-growing cities are in southern England
  • Hybrid working arrangements are enabling buyers to consider locations further from traditional workplaces
  • Areas like Salford and Bradford are attracting buyers priced out of nearby major cities

As the UK property market continues to evolve, these regional disparities in price growth appear set to persist, with affordability remaining a key driver of buyer behaviour. Homeowners and investors might consider how these long-term trends could influence future property values across different parts of the country.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Northern Cities Lead UK House Price Growth as London Lags Behind

New analysis reveals a striking regional divide in UK property price growth over the past decade, with Manchester emerging as the standout performer while London trails behind. According to Rightmove data, average asking prices in Manchester have surged by 63% since 2014, compared to just 7% growth in the capital. This widening gap highlights how affordability and shifting work patterns are reshaping Britain's housing market.

The North-South Price Growth Divide

The research shows none of the top 10 cities for house price growth are located in southern England, with Greater Manchester communities dominating the list. Four areas - Levenshulme, Atherton, Droylsden and Failsworth - have each seen prices climb around 80% since 2016. Other strong performers include Wolverhampton, Newport and Nottingham, all benefiting from their relative affordability compared to southern counterparts.

Several factors appear to be driving this trend:

  • More buyers seeking value beyond traditional expensive urban centres
  • A "spillover" effect as purchasers look to neighbouring towns and cities
  • Changing work patterns reducing the need to live near major employment hubs

London's Slower Growth Trajectory

While London retains the UK's highest average asking prices at £687,080, its modest 7% growth over the decade suggests the capital's market may be approaching affordability limits. Rightmove's analysis indicates that with prices already at elevated levels, London has less capacity for further significant increases compared to more affordable regions.

The data also reveals:

  • Five of the 10 slowest-growing cities are in southern England
  • Hybrid working arrangements are enabling buyers to consider locations further from traditional workplaces
  • Areas like Salford and Bradford are attracting buyers priced out of nearby major cities

As the UK property market continues to evolve, these regional disparities in price growth appear set to persist, with affordability remaining a key driver of buyer behaviour. Homeowners and investors might consider how these long-term trends could influence future property values across different parts of the country.

item.title: A fool proof stamp duty savings scheme… hidden in plain sight
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Stamp Duty Land Tax (SDLT) remains one of Britain's most contentious property taxes, affecting everyone from first-time buyers to international investors purchasing multimillion-pound mansions. With rates reaching as high as 19% for some overseas buyers, homeowners may wish to explore legitimate ways to mitigate these costs whilst remaining fully compliant with HMRC rules.

Understanding the true cost of SDLT

Since the 2014 reforms, SDLT has become increasingly complex, with additional surcharges for second homes, buy-to-let properties and non-UK residents. The current structure means:

  • Non-residents purchasing a £20m property could face a £3.8m SDLT bill
  • Additional 2% surcharge for non-UK buyers on top of standard rates
  • Higher rates applying to entire purchase price bands, not just amounts above thresholds

These substantial upfront costs come before considering ongoing maintenance expenses for high-value properties, which can easily exceed £500,000 annually for large estates with extensive facilities.

Potential strategies to consider

Whilst SDLT avoidance is illegal, there are legitimate planning opportunities that could reduce liabilities:

  • Exploring mixed-use property classifications where commercial elements may qualify for lower rates
  • Considering phased purchase structures for development projects
  • Reviewing potential reliefs such as multiple dwellings relief where applicable
  • Timing purchases to align with temporary SDLT holidays or threshold changes

Property investors might also weigh SDLT costs against other factors like location, potential rental yields and long-term capital growth prospects when making purchasing decisions.

With careful planning and professional advice, buyers could potentially navigate the SDLT landscape more efficiently. However, it remains crucial to ensure all arrangements comply fully with HMRC regulations to avoid penalties or investigations.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Stamp Duty Land Tax (SDLT) remains one of Britain's most contentious property taxes, affecting everyone from first-time buyers to international investors purchasing multimillion-pound mansions. With rates reaching as high as 19% for some overseas buyers, homeowners may wish to explore legitimate ways to mitigate these costs whilst remaining fully compliant with HMRC rules.

Understanding the true cost of SDLT

Since the 2014 reforms, SDLT has become increasingly complex, with additional surcharges for second homes, buy-to-let properties and non-UK residents. The current structure means:

  • Non-residents purchasing a £20m property could face a £3.8m SDLT bill
  • Additional 2% surcharge for non-UK buyers on top of standard rates
  • Higher rates applying to entire purchase price bands, not just amounts above thresholds

These substantial upfront costs come before considering ongoing maintenance expenses for high-value properties, which can easily exceed £500,000 annually for large estates with extensive facilities.

Potential strategies to consider

Whilst SDLT avoidance is illegal, there are legitimate planning opportunities that could reduce liabilities:

  • Exploring mixed-use property classifications where commercial elements may qualify for lower rates
  • Considering phased purchase structures for development projects
  • Reviewing potential reliefs such as multiple dwellings relief where applicable
  • Timing purchases to align with temporary SDLT holidays or threshold changes

Property investors might also weigh SDLT costs against other factors like location, potential rental yields and long-term capital growth prospects when making purchasing decisions.

With careful planning and professional advice, buyers could potentially navigate the SDLT landscape more efficiently. However, it remains crucial to ensure all arrangements comply fully with HMRC regulations to avoid penalties or investigations.

item.title: Main cause of tenancy deposit disputes revealed
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Cleaning remains the most frequent cause of disputes between landlords and tenants when it comes to tenancy deposits, according to the latest figures from The Deposit Protection Service (DPS). In 2025, cleaning-related issues accounted for nearly 30% of all deposit claims, marking the fifth consecutive year it has topped the list of disagreements. Damage to property and rent arrears followed, making up 18.42% and 16.45% of claims respectively.

Why Cleaning Causes the Most Disputes

Cleaning has consistently been the primary source of contention at the end of a tenancy. Landlords often expect properties to be returned in a spotless condition, whilst tenants may feel they have left the property sufficiently clean. This mismatch in expectations can lead to disputes over deductions from the deposit. Under tenancy deposit rules, landlords are entitled to deduct reasonable costs to cover any cleaning expenses incurred after a tenant moves out.

Matt Trevett, Managing Director of The DPS, emphasised the importance of clear communication between landlords and tenants. He noted that understanding obligations at the end of a tenancy can help reduce disagreements. “Less than 5% of all deposits protected by The DPS end in a dispute,” he added, highlighting the impartial dispute resolution service available to both parties when an agreement cannot be reached.

Other Common Causes of Deposit Disputes

Whilst cleaning dominates the list, damage to property and rent arrears are also significant contributors to deposit disputes. Damage claims often arise from wear and tear beyond what is considered reasonable, whilst rent arrears disputes occur when tenants fail to pay their rent in full before vacating the property.

Interestingly, disputes related to redecoration have seen a steady increase over the past five years, rising by 3.4 percentage points since 2021. This suggests that landlords and tenants may have differing views on what constitutes acceptable alterations or changes to a property’s appearance during a tenancy.

How to Avoid Deposit Disputes

Tenants may wish to take proactive steps to minimise the risk of deposit disputes. Ensuring the property is left in a clean and undamaged condition, as per the tenancy agreement, can help avoid disagreements. It could also be worth documenting the property’s condition at both the start and end of the tenancy through photographs or videos.

On the other hand, landlords might consider providing tenants with a clear checklist of their expectations before the tenancy ends. Open communication can go a long way in preventing misunderstandings and ensuring a smoother transition for both parties.

Whilst disputes are relatively rare, understanding the common causes and taking preventative measures can help landlords and tenants navigate the end of a tenancy more harmoniously.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Cleaning remains the most frequent cause of disputes between landlords and tenants when it comes to tenancy deposits, according to the latest figures from The Deposit Protection Service (DPS). In 2025, cleaning-related issues accounted for nearly 30% of all deposit claims, marking the fifth consecutive year it has topped the list of disagreements. Damage to property and rent arrears followed, making up 18.42% and 16.45% of claims respectively.

Why Cleaning Causes the Most Disputes

Cleaning has consistently been the primary source of contention at the end of a tenancy. Landlords often expect properties to be returned in a spotless condition, whilst tenants may feel they have left the property sufficiently clean. This mismatch in expectations can lead to disputes over deductions from the deposit. Under tenancy deposit rules, landlords are entitled to deduct reasonable costs to cover any cleaning expenses incurred after a tenant moves out.

Matt Trevett, Managing Director of The DPS, emphasised the importance of clear communication between landlords and tenants. He noted that understanding obligations at the end of a tenancy can help reduce disagreements. “Less than 5% of all deposits protected by The DPS end in a dispute,” he added, highlighting the impartial dispute resolution service available to both parties when an agreement cannot be reached.

Other Common Causes of Deposit Disputes

Whilst cleaning dominates the list, damage to property and rent arrears are also significant contributors to deposit disputes. Damage claims often arise from wear and tear beyond what is considered reasonable, whilst rent arrears disputes occur when tenants fail to pay their rent in full before vacating the property.

Interestingly, disputes related to redecoration have seen a steady increase over the past five years, rising by 3.4 percentage points since 2021. This suggests that landlords and tenants may have differing views on what constitutes acceptable alterations or changes to a property’s appearance during a tenancy.

How to Avoid Deposit Disputes

Tenants may wish to take proactive steps to minimise the risk of deposit disputes. Ensuring the property is left in a clean and undamaged condition, as per the tenancy agreement, can help avoid disagreements. It could also be worth documenting the property’s condition at both the start and end of the tenancy through photographs or videos.

On the other hand, landlords might consider providing tenants with a clear checklist of their expectations before the tenancy ends. Open communication can go a long way in preventing misunderstandings and ensuring a smoother transition for both parties.

Whilst disputes are relatively rare, understanding the common causes and taking preventative measures can help landlords and tenants navigate the end of a tenancy more harmoniously.

item.title: Biggest June house price drop in 14 years
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New data from Rightmove has revealed the largest June house price drop in 14 years, as an increase in property supply and softer demand from buyers shifts the balance of negotiating power. This significant dip highlights changing dynamics in the UK housing market, offering potential opportunities for those looking to move or invest.

What’s Driving the Drop?

Several factors have contributed to this notable decline in house prices. Firstly, the supply of available properties has risen, giving buyers more options to choose from. Additionally, demand has softened, partly due to higher mortgage rates and broader economic uncertainty. These conditions have created a more buyer-friendly market, where negotiation leverage has shifted in favour of purchasers.

This trend marks a departure from the intense competition seen in recent years, where limited stock often led to bidding wars and rapid price growth. With more homes on the market and fewer buyers actively searching, sellers may need to adjust their expectations to secure a sale.

Implications for Buyers and Sellers

For prospective buyers, this shift could present an opportunity to secure a property at a more favourable price. With increased negotiating power, buyers might find it easier to agree on a deal that aligns with their budget. However, it’s worth noting that mortgage rates remain higher than in previous years, so affordability should be carefully assessed.

On the other hand, sellers may need to adopt a more realistic approach to pricing. While the market remains active, properties priced competitively are more likely to attract interest. Homeowners considering selling might benefit from seeking professional advice to ensure their pricing strategy reflects current market conditions.

The UK housing market continues to evolve, with this June’s price drop highlighting the importance of staying informed. Whether buying or selling, understanding these trends can help individuals make more informed decisions in a changing landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New data from Rightmove has revealed the largest June house price drop in 14 years, as an increase in property supply and softer demand from buyers shifts the balance of negotiating power. This significant dip highlights changing dynamics in the UK housing market, offering potential opportunities for those looking to move or invest.

What’s Driving the Drop?

Several factors have contributed to this notable decline in house prices. Firstly, the supply of available properties has risen, giving buyers more options to choose from. Additionally, demand has softened, partly due to higher mortgage rates and broader economic uncertainty. These conditions have created a more buyer-friendly market, where negotiation leverage has shifted in favour of purchasers.

This trend marks a departure from the intense competition seen in recent years, where limited stock often led to bidding wars and rapid price growth. With more homes on the market and fewer buyers actively searching, sellers may need to adjust their expectations to secure a sale.

Implications for Buyers and Sellers

For prospective buyers, this shift could present an opportunity to secure a property at a more favourable price. With increased negotiating power, buyers might find it easier to agree on a deal that aligns with their budget. However, it’s worth noting that mortgage rates remain higher than in previous years, so affordability should be carefully assessed.

On the other hand, sellers may need to adopt a more realistic approach to pricing. While the market remains active, properties priced competitively are more likely to attract interest. Homeowners considering selling might benefit from seeking professional advice to ensure their pricing strategy reflects current market conditions.

The UK housing market continues to evolve, with this June’s price drop highlighting the importance of staying informed. Whether buying or selling, understanding these trends can help individuals make more informed decisions in a changing landscape.

item.title: Residential property no longer reliable retirement investment
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Recent analysis has cast doubt on residential property's traditional position as a dependable retirement investment, with data showing it has delivered weaker returns than both equities and inflation over comparable periods.

Property underperformance raises questions

Whilst bricks and mortar have long been considered a cornerstone of retirement planning, fresh research indicates UK residential property has struggled to keep pace with alternative investments. The findings suggest homeowners relying solely on property wealth might need to reconsider their long-term financial strategies.

Key factors contributing to this trend include:

  • Slower house price growth in many regions compared to historical averages
  • Increasing maintenance costs and taxation changes affecting net returns
  • Stock market investments outperforming property over multiple timeframes

Diversification considerations for retirement planning

Financial experts increasingly emphasise the importance of diversified portfolios rather than over-reliance on any single asset class. Those approaching retirement might wish to explore:

  • Spreading investments across different asset types
  • Considering pension products alongside property ownership
  • Reviewing inheritance tax implications of different investment approaches

The research doesn't suggest property has lost all value as an investment, but rather highlights the potential risks of depending on it exclusively for retirement income. As with all financial decisions, seeking professional advice tailored to individual circumstances remains crucial.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Recent analysis has cast doubt on residential property's traditional position as a dependable retirement investment, with data showing it has delivered weaker returns than both equities and inflation over comparable periods.

Property underperformance raises questions

Whilst bricks and mortar have long been considered a cornerstone of retirement planning, fresh research indicates UK residential property has struggled to keep pace with alternative investments. The findings suggest homeowners relying solely on property wealth might need to reconsider their long-term financial strategies.

Key factors contributing to this trend include:

  • Slower house price growth in many regions compared to historical averages
  • Increasing maintenance costs and taxation changes affecting net returns
  • Stock market investments outperforming property over multiple timeframes

Diversification considerations for retirement planning

Financial experts increasingly emphasise the importance of diversified portfolios rather than over-reliance on any single asset class. Those approaching retirement might wish to explore:

  • Spreading investments across different asset types
  • Considering pension products alongside property ownership
  • Reviewing inheritance tax implications of different investment approaches

The research doesn't suggest property has lost all value as an investment, but rather highlights the potential risks of depending on it exclusively for retirement income. As with all financial decisions, seeking professional advice tailored to individual circumstances remains crucial.
